title: Nexus / Discovery Engine
public_claim: A local research-development application with two linked halves: cross-field mechanism discovery and dependency-aware academic workflow.
distinctive_mechanism: The discovery side searches for transferable mechanisms from other fields and tests whether they survive context, language, timing, and analogy limits; Observatory, Scanner, Bridge, Cross-Pollination Lab, Assay Lab, Crucible, Incubator, Forge, and the Galaxy Map make those cross-field mechanism relations visible. The academic side carries problem exploration, problem statement, literature review, methodology, analysis, discussion, conclusion, and editing through stage rooms with instruments and standards; upstream changes can mark downstream stages stale, and downstream revisions can force.

flagship_demos.title: Nexus cross-field mechanism transfer
flagship_demos.why_distinctive: Most search systems retrieve adjacent terms. This demo asks whether the system can carry a mechanism from one field into another while preserving fit pressure, analogy limits, and downstream research-workflow consequences.

fixture.initial_research_question: How can analysts detect low-rate evidence corruption in retrieval-supported intelligence workflows before it becomes a confident analytic claim?
fixture.source_field: layered air defense and mixed-speed salvo management
fixture.target_field: retrieval integrity and analyst review burden
fixture.mechanism_family: cheap decoys saturate scarce high-grade defensive resources while slower contamination changes the operating environment over time

runbook: Enter the question into Nexus Intake and Observatory.
Use Galaxy Map to locate non-obvious adjacent mechanism fields rather than keyword-neighbor papers only.
Create Bridge mapping from mixed-speed air-defense logic to retrieval poisoning.
Run XPol fit pressure: timing, resource asymmetry, false analogy, and boundary conditions.
Route surviving hypothesis into Forge with a claim ceiling and stale-state dependencies.
expected_evidence: Bridge mapping with at least one rejected false analogy
Forge-ready research object with source and stale-state links
acceptance_criteria: The final hypothesis is not a mere metaphor; it names a transferable mechanism.
At least one analogy limit is explicitly rejected or bounded.
A downstream stage records what must be rechecked if the problem statement or method changes.

flagship_demos.title: Nexus academic upstream/downstream stale-state propagation
flagship_demos.why_distinctive: The value is not only moving through research stages; it is forcing later stages to reopen when earlier foundations change.

fixture.original_problem_statement: AI-assisted analysts resist corrupted evidence under structured prompting.
fixture.revised_problem_statement: Human-AI teams distribute detection burden differently across corruption families.

runbook: Create a project with a problem statement, methodology, and analysis stage.
Change the problem statement after later stages exist.
Inspect which stages become stale or review-required.
Attempt Forge release before stale stages are resolved.
expected_evidence: Dependency map showing why later sections require review
acceptance_criteria: A downstream stage cannot silently remain release-ready after a material upstream change.
The system distinguishes minor wording edits from changes affecting method or findings.
